common_name: Desert Willow (Flor de Mimbre, Willowleaf Catalpa)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, sandy or rocky, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; desert willow is native to dry arroyos, stream banks, and desert washes of the Chihuahuan and Sonoran Deserts and is extremely drought tolerant — it actually performs better with no supplemental irrigation after the first year.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert willow is one of the most beautiful flowering native trees of the Southwest and South Texas, producing masses of showy, orchid-like flowers in pink, lavender, rose, white, or bicolor from late spring through fall in multiple flushes — a spectacular hummingbird and butterfly plant. Despite its name it is not a willow at all but rather a member of the Bignonia family, closely related to catalpa. The long, willow-like leaves emerge late in spring and drop early in fall. It is remarkably drought tolerant once established, thriving on natural rainfall alone in zone 7–11. The decorative seed pods hang on through winter. Many named cultivars are available with different flower colors and forms; 'Dark Storm,' 'Bubba,' and 'Art's Seedless' are popular selections for southern gardens.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; globba emerges from dormancy in late spring and blooms through summer and fall. In zone 8–9, mulch rhizomes heavily before the first frost to protect from freeze.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; dancing ladies ginger is one of the most delightful and enchanting of all the ornamental gingers, producing pendant clusters of small mauve-purple flowers with reflexed wings and a long, curved, yellow lip that resembles tiny ballerinas or dancing ladies when the breeze sets them in motion. The nodding flower clusters 2–4 inches long appear on arching stems from summer through fall. It is one of the most shade-tolerant of all the ornamental gingers and is an excellent candidate for bringing color to shaded tropical gardens from summer through fall. In zone 8–9 it often produces small bulbils (little bulb-like structures) in the leaf axils that can be separated and planted to propagate new plants. Combine with ferns, caladiums, and impatiens in the shaded border.
sources: American Hosta and Shade Plant Society

planting.note: Root 3–4 inch stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix; cuttings root in 1–2 weeks and can be transplanted to beds or containers. In zone 9–11, establish in spring for summer groundcover coverage. Keep soil moist and provide light shade to partial shade for best foliage color.

directions: Root cuttings in water and transplant to moist, well-drained soil in partial shade; Tradescantia zebrina (wandering jew) is one of the fastest-spreading and most colorful of all tropical groundcovers for shaded areas, producing a mat of trailing stems with striking elliptical leaves in deep green and silver stripes with brilliant purple undersides — the tricolor effect is particularly vivid in good light. Small pink-purple three-petaled flowers are produced nearly year-round. In zone 9–11 it spreads freely as a low groundcover under trees and in shaded planting beds, rooting wherever stems touch moist soil; it can be aggressive and should be kept away from natural areas. For containers, the trailing stems are beautiful in hanging baskets on covered porches. The contact sap may cause contact dermatitis in some people; handle with caution. Easy to propagate from cuttings and nearly impossible to kill.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean, dry to moderately dry soil in partial shade; white mistflower is native to limestone hills and canyon slopes in south-central Texas and northeastern Mexico and thrives in rocky alkaline conditions where few other plants survive.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; white mistflower is one of the most spectacular fall-blooming native shrubs of the Texas Hill Country and Gulf South, covering itself in dense clusters of pure white, fluffy, ageratum-like flowers in October–November just when most other plants are going dormant. The fragrant flowers are outstanding for butterflies and late-season pollinators, including migrating monarch butterflies that use them as a critical nectar source during the fall migration. The plant is semi-evergreen in mild winters. It grows naturally in the shade of limestone canyon walls and cedars, making it one of the more shade-tolerant native fall-blooming shrubs available. The common name 'mistflower' refers to the soft, misty appearance of the white flower clusters. Cut back by one-third in early spring to encourage compact, dense growth.

common_name: Purple Heart (Purple Queen, Purple Wandering Jew)

planting.note: Root 3–4 inch stem cuttings in water or moist mix in spring; purple heart roots easily and quickly. Plant in any well-drained soil from full sun to partial shade. Full sun intensifies the deep purple color; shade causes the stems to elongate and the color to fade to green-purple. In zone 7–8 tops die back in frost; roots survive to zone 7.

directions: Root cuttings in water and plant in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; purple heart is one of the most valuable and widely grown groundcovers for Gulf Coast gardens, producing thick, spreading mats of deep, intensely violet-purple succulent stems and strap-like leaves with a metallic sheen. The color is among the most saturated purple of any plant — a striking design element in the landscape. It produces small pink flowers in summer. Purple heart is nearly indestructible — tolerating drought, heat, salt spray, poor soil, and reflected heat from pavement. In zone 7–11 it is perennial (tops die back in freezes but resprout); it can also be overwintered indoors as a container plant or trailing houseplant. An outstanding container plant for providing dramatic color contrast with silver, chartreuse, or orange-flowering companions. Deer resistant.

common_name: Copper Canyon Daisy (Mountain Marigold, Lemmon's Marigold)

planting.note: Root tip cuttings easily in spring or plant container-grown transplants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; copper canyon daisy is native to Copper Canyon (Barranca del Cobre) in Mexico and is drought tolerant once established. Prune by one-third after bloom flushes to maintain compact shape.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; copper canyon daisy is one of the finest large perennial flowering shrubs for zone 8–11 gardens — a sprawling, aromatic shrub covered in masses of small, bright golden-yellow daisy flowers in fall and winter (and sometimes spring) when very little else is blooming. The deeply divided, fernlike leaves are pungently aromatic (a strong citrus-mint-marigold scent that is powerfully deer repellent) and retain an attractive gray-green color through much of the year. It is outstanding for fall color, butterfly attraction, and combining with blue salvias or purple ageratum for a stunning gold-and-purple fall combination. Prune back by one-third after each bloom flush to keep the plant compact; an unpruned shrub can spread to 6 feet or more. In zone 8, top growth may be killed by hard freezes but roots resprout reliably.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; flame acanthus is native to the limestone hills and brushlands of south-central Texas and northeastern Mexico and is extremely drought tolerant and heat tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; flame acanthus (hummingbird bush) earns its name with brilliant orange-red tubular flowers that literally glow like flames and attract hummingbirds from the moment the plants begin blooming in late spring through fall. It is a tough, deciduous or semi-evergreen native shrub of the Texas brush country that thrives in rocky alkaline soil, extreme heat, and drought conditions that would wilt most plants. The orange-scarlet flowers are produced in profusion from spring through frost, providing hummingbirds with a nearly continuous nectar source. It is deer resistant and rabbit resistant. The plant dies to the ground in hard freezes in zone 7–8 but resprouts vigorously from established root systems. An essential and easy plant for the wildlife-friendly Gulf Coast garden.

common_name: Texas Olive (Mexican Olive, Anacahuita, Wild Olive)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Texas olive is native to extreme south Texas and northeastern Mexico and prefers alkaline, calcareous soils. It is tolerant of clay and caliche but requires excellent drainage and is damaged at 22–25°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas olive is one of the most spectacular native flowering trees of the Rio Grande Plains and an outstanding ornamental for zone 8–11 gardens. The large, tropical-looking leathery leaves and stunning pure white, 2-inch funnel-shaped flowers with a yellow throat are produced year-round in warm weather, attracting bees, butterflies, and birds. The olive-like fruits ripen to white and are eaten by mockingbirds and other wildlife. The tree is semi-evergreen, holding its large velvety leaves in mild winters and dropping them only in cold or drought. It is highly drought tolerant once established and grows naturally on rocky limestone slopes, chaparral, and thorny brush country of the Rio Grande Plains. In zone 8a it may be killed to the ground in severe winters but resprouting from roots is common.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade; frostweed grows naturally in moist woodland edges, creek bottoms, and open woods from the eastern US to Texas and tolerates considerable shade while still producing flowers.

directions: Sow seeds in fall or plant transplants in moist, partially shaded woodland-edge soil; frostweed is a tall, commanding native perennial wildflower that earns its evocative name from a remarkable winter phenomenon: when the first hard freeze strikes the green stems, the sap freezes and expands, splitting the bark and extruding curling ribbons and sheets of ice in fantastical flower-like formations called 'ice flowers' or 'frost flowers' that persist for hours after sunrise. This natural ice art is one of the most spectacular surprises in the winter garden. The plant itself produces masses of small white flowers beloved by butterflies — particularly migrating monarchs — from late summer through fall. It is an important nectar source at a critical migration season. Tolerates part shade and moist conditions, making it one of the few native tall perennials suited to moist woodland edges and rain gardens.

common_name: Silver Ponyfoot (Silver Falls Dichondra, Silver Nickel Vine)

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist mix or plant container transplants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver ponyfoot requires excellent drainage and actually thrives in poor, rocky, dry soils where other groundcovers fail. It is sensitive to overwatering and cold below 25°F.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry soil in full sun; silver ponyfoot (Dichondra argentea) is a stunning silvery native groundcover and container trailing plant, native to dry rocky slopes of south Texas, New Mexico, and Mexico. The small, round, kidney-shaped leaves are densely covered in fine silver hairs that give the plant its brilliant silver color — one of the most intensely silver of any plant, creating a shimmering pool of metallic light in the garden. It is spectacular trailing from containers and hanging baskets, where the silver cascading stems create a waterfall effect. As a groundcover it stays low (2–4 inches) and spreads to cover rocky, dry areas. It thrives in heat and drought, requiring little to no irrigation once established. Excellent for edging, rock gardens, and container combos where its silver tone contrasts beautifully with deep purple or lime-green companions.
